questions.text: Which statement BEST captures the Web2 trap?

options.text: A website that crashes when too many users visit at the same time.
A centralised platform that locks in providers and consumers, then extracts an ever increasing commission while denying them ownership of their data, reputation, and customer relationships.
A government regulation that forces apps to pay higher taxes.
A marketing trick where ads are disguised as genuine recommendations.

questions.explanation: The Web2 trap is an economic structure, not a technical failure (A), a regulatory issue (C), or a marketing tactic (D). Option B captures the full lifecycle: platform inserts itself as intermediary, locks in both sides, and raises the rent. This is a definition question worth 1 mark.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Explanation

The Web2 trap is an economic structure, not a technical failure (A), a regulatory issue (C), or a marketing tactic (D). Option B captures the full lifecycle: platform inserts itself as intermediary, locks in both sides, and raises the rent. This is a definition question worth 1 mark.

Exam tip: When a question asks for “best description”, look for the option that covers cause, mechanism, and consequence.

questions.text: A cloud kitchen sells a dish for ₹280 on Swiggy. Swiggy's commission is 25% + GST (18% on commission), and the packaging costs ₹20. What is the kitchen's net revenue from this order (before ingredient costs)?
questions.explanation: Commission = 25% of ₹280 = ₹70. GST on commission = 18% of ₹70 = ₹12.60. Packaging = ₹20. Total deductions = ₹70 + ₹12.60 + ₹20 = ₹102.60. Net revenue = ₹280 - ₹102.60 = ₹177.40. This is a straightforward calculation with a clear step by step - 2 marks for correct application.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Calculation

Commission = 25% of ₹280 = ₹70. GST on commission = 18% of ₹70 = ₹12.60. Packaging = ₹20. Total deductions = ₹70 + ₹12.60 + ₹20 = ₹102.60. Net revenue = ₹280 - ₹102.60 = ₹177.40. This is a straightforward calculation with a clear step by step - 2 marks for correct application.

Common mistake: Applying GST on the total order value instead of only the commission.

questions.text: An Amazon seller complains: “I have sold 5,000 units, but I cannot contact a single buyer to inform them about my new product line.” Which lever of the Web2 trap does this illustrate?
questions.explanation: The platform's refusal to share customer contact information is a data moat. The seller generated value but cannot build an independent business because the platform withholds the customer relationship. This is not about fees (A), visibility (C), or account deletion (D).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Explanation

The platform's refusal to share customer contact information is a data moat. The seller generated value but cannot build an independent business because the platform withholds the customer relationship. This is not about fees (A), visibility (C), or account deletion (D).

Real world link: The CCI's investigation into Amazon and Flipkart specifically cites data hoarding as anti competitive.

questions.text: A ride hailing platform sets “surge pricing” automatically during peak hours. Drivers have no visibility into how the surge multiplier is calculated, and the platform takes the majority of the surge revenue. Which two levers are at play here?
questions.explanation: Algorithmic control - the surge formula is a black box controlled entirely by the platform. Commission extraction - the platform takes a percentage of the inflated surge fare, so its absolute commission rises. The two levers work together to maximise platform revenue at the driver's expense.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

Algorithmic control - the surge formula is a black box controlled entirely by the platform. Commission extraction - the platform takes a percentage of the inflated surge fare, so its absolute commission rises. The two levers work together to maximise platform revenue at the driver's expense.

Key insight: The levers often reinforce each other. Understanding their interplay is critical for both the assessment and for designing Web3 alternatives.

questions.text: In a Web3 food delivery protocol on MST, how would the relationship between a restaurant and a customer fundamentally differ from the Web2 model?

options.text: The app would have a nicer UI with better animations and a dark mode.
The protocol would charge exactly the same commission but donate it to a charity chosen by the customer.
The customer would own their identity and data, the restaurant could interact with them directly (with consent), and both would have governance rights in the protocol through token ownership.
There would be no fees whatsoever - blockchain makes everything free.

questions.explanation: The Web3 difference is architectural, not cosmetic (A) or philanthropic (B). Option C captures the three core shifts: (1) Self sovereign identity - customers control their data. (2) Direct relationship - no gatekeeper between restaurant and customer. (3) Token based governance - users and providers vote on protocol rules. Option D is false - blockchain has gas fees, but they are orders of magnitude smaller than platform commissions. This synthesis question tests whether you understand that Web3 is about ownership and governance, not just lower cost. Worth 3 marks.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Explanation

The Web3 difference is architectural, not cosmetic (A) or philanthropic (B). Option C captures the three core shifts: (1) Self sovereign identity - customers control their data. (2) Direct relationship - no gatekeeper between restaurant and customer. (3) Token based governance - users and providers vote on protocol rules. Option D is false - blockchain has gas fees, but they are orders of magnitude smaller than platform commissions. This synthesis question tests whether you understand that Web3 is about ownership and governance, not just lower cost. Worth 3 marks.

Developer's lens: When designing a Web3 dApp, your job is not to remove all costs - it is to ensure that costs are transparent, minimal, and governed by the community.
